questions.question: Explain the difference between ios::app and ios::ate file modes in C++ file handling. Also, explain why it is important to explicitly close a file using close() function and what happens if files are not closed properly.
keyPoints: ios::app moves the put pointer to end of file before each write, preserving existing content
ios::ate moves get pointer to end of file initially but allows writing at any position
Explicit closing flushes buffer to ensure all data is written to disk
Unclosed files may cause data loss, resource leaks and undefined behavior

questions.question: Write a C++ program to open a file named 'data.txt' in input mode and check for errors using fail() and eof() functions. Explain the purpose of these error checking functions and when each should be used.
keyPoints: fail() returns true when stream enters error state due to invalid operation or format error
eof() returns true only when end-of-file is reached during extraction
Use fail() to check if file opening succeeded before processing
Always check both eof() and fail() in loop conditions to avoid processing invalid data

questions.question: Write a C++ program that demonstrates file positioning functions seekg() and seekp(). Create a file 'numbers.dat' containing integers from 1 to 10. Then: (a) Use seekp() to modify the 5th integer to 99, (b) Use seekg() to read and display all integers from the beginning, (c) Use seekg() to move to the 7th position and read its value. Explain the difference between seekg() and seekp() and how file positioning works in text vs binary mode.
keyPoints: seekg() moves the get (read) pointer, seekp() moves the put (write) pointer
In binary mode, seekg(offset, dir) moves pointer by offset bytes from specified direction
In text mode, only zero offset with ios::beg is guaranteed to work reliably
Always check return value of seekg/seekp using stream object's boolean conversion

questions.question: Write a C++ program to create a binary file 'student.dat' to store student records (containing name, roll number, and marks). Implement functions to: (a) Add new student records using ios::app and ios::binary modes, (b) Read and display all student records, (c) Search for a student by roll number using random access with seekg(). Explain the advantages of binary file I/O over text file I/O and why ios::binary is essential when handling binary data.
keyPoints: ios::binary prevents automatic conversion of newline characters (\n to \r\n on Windows)
Binary I/O reads/writes exact byte representation without formatting overhead
Use read()/write() member functions with char* buffer for binary operations
sizeof() operator essential to determine number of bytes to read/write for structures
